techniques.name: Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment
techniques.evidence: During the intrusion, the threat actor gained initial access by sending a spearphishing email containing a malicious Microsoft Word attachment.

techniques.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell
techniques.evidence: an embedded VBA macro executed that decoded a base64 string and launched PowerShell with the -EncodedCommand flag to run a second-stage payload entirely in memory.

techniques.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
techniques.evidence: The PowerShell stage downloaded an additional implant over HTTPS from an attacker-controlled domain.

techniques.name: Boot or Logon Initialization Scripts: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
techniques.evidence: The implant established persistence by creating a new entry in the Run registry key (HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run) that pointed to the implant binary.

techniques.name: OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory
techniques.evidence: For credential access, the actor used Mimikatz on the compromised host to dump credentials from LSASS memory.

techniques.name: Remote Services: SMB/Windows Admin Shares
techniques.evidence: They then moved laterally across the network using valid domain administrator credentials and the Server Message Block protocol to copy tooling to remote hosts.

techniques.name: Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols
techniques.evidence: Command and control traffic was carried over HTTPS to a small number of attacker-controlled web servers, blending in with normal web browsing traffic.

techniques.name: Archive Collected Data
techniques.evidence: Prior to exfiltration, the actor compressed staged data into a multi-volume RAR archive on a staging host.

techniques.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
techniques.evidence: Exfiltration was then performed over the same HTTPS C2 channel.
